title: How a Four-Stroke Engine Works
prompt: How does a four-stroke engine work?

explanation.written: The prompt 'How does a four-stroke engine work?' is asking for an explanation of a mechanism's working principle. A good answer reveals what's inside the engine block (you can't explain a four-stroke without showing the piston, valves and crank), shows the four strokes in motion (Intake → Compression → Power → Exhaust — the timing is the point), and labels the parts in place so the user can tie what they see to the names they'll hear elsewhere.

mechanics.why: Reveal the interior so the moving parts are visible — a four-stroke is unintelligible from the outside.
Cut the block along the cylinder axis so the user can see the cylinder, piston, valves, and head at once.
Loop the four strokes so the user can see the timing relationships between piston, valves, and crank.

items.label: Cylinder
items.finding: The piston rides up and down inside this bore.

items.label: Piston
items.finding: Converts gas pressure into linear motion.

items.label: Intake valve
items.finding: Opens during the Intake stroke to let the air/fuel mix in.

items.label: Exhaust valve
items.finding: Opens during the Exhaust stroke to let burnt gas out.

items.label: Spark plug
items.finding: Fires at top-dead-centre on the Power stroke.

items.label: Crankshaft
items.finding: Translates piston motion into rotation — this is where torque leaves the engine.

mechanics.why: Pin the part names so the user can tie what they see to the vocabulary.

sources.title: Four strokes, in order
items: Intake — piston down, intake valve open, exhaust valve shut. Air-fuel mix fills the cylinder.
Compression — both valves shut. Piston rises, squeezing the charge.
Power — spark plug fires at top of stroke. Combustion drives the piston down — this is where torque is produced.
Exhaust — exhaust valve opens, piston rises and pushes burnt gas out.
